questions.question: Section 3(1) of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 creates the offence of assault causing harm. What are the two essential elements that the prosecution must prove?

options.A: That the accused applied force to the complainant, and that the force was intentional
options.B: That the accused committed an assault within the meaning of s.2, and that the assault caused 'harm' as defined in s.1 NFOATP 1997
options.C: That the accused caused serious physical injury to the complainant, and that the injury was permanent
options.D: That the accused threatened force and caused psychological harm, and that both resulted in hospitalisation

questions.explanation: Section 3(1) NFOATP 1997 is a two-element offence: (1) The accused must have committed an assault within the meaning of s.2 (either physical application of force or threatened assault); AND (2) That assault must have caused 'harm' as defined in s.1 NFOATP 1997. Both elements must be independently proved — this was confirmed in Minister for Justice v Donnelly [2012] IEHC. 'Harm' under s.1 is defined broadly: harm to the body or mind, including pain, unconsciousness, and substantial impairment of physical or mental condition. It does not require serious or permanent injury. Option A omits the harm element. Option C applies the 'serious harm' threshold belonging to s.4. Option D mischaracterises the elements and adds a hospitalisation requirement that does not exist.

questions.question: Section 1 NFOATP 1997 defines 'harm' for the purposes of s.3. Which of the following best states that definition?

options.A: 'Harm' means grievous bodily harm — it must be serious, substantial, and more than transient
options.B: 'Harm' means harm to the body or mind, including pain, unconsciousness, and substantial impairment of physical or mental condition — there is no requirement that the harm be permanent or serious
options.C: 'Harm' means any form of physical injury visible to the naked eye
options.D: 'Harm' means hospitalisation or treatment by a medical professional

questions.explanation: Section 1 NFOATP 1997 defines 'harm' as harm to the body or mind of a person, including pain or unconsciousness, or substantial loss or impairment of the function of a bodily member or organ. Critically, the definition does not require the harm to be permanent, serious, or visible. Even temporary pain or psychological distress can constitute 'harm' under s.1. This broad definition distinguishes s.3 'harm' from 'serious harm' under s.4 (which requires substantial risk of death, serious disfigurement, or substantial loss of mobility or bodily function). Option A imports the English 'grievous bodily harm' concept which does not apply in Irish law under s.3. Option C incorrectly requires visible injury. Option D invents a hospitalisation requirement that has no statutory basis.

questions.question: What is the maximum sentence for assault causing harm under s.3 NFOATP 1997 on conviction on indictment following the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023?

options.A: 5 years imprisonment — unchanged since the 1997 Act
options.B: 7 years imprisonment — amended by the Courts Act 2013
options.C: 10 years imprisonment — the maximum was doubled from 5 years by s.20 of the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023, which came into force on 1 November 2023
options.D: Life imprisonment — assault causing harm is treated as equivalent to serious harm

questions.explanation: This is an exam-critical point. Prior to 1 November 2023, the maximum sentence for s.3 assault causing harm on conviction on indictment was 5 years. Section 20 of the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2023 (in force 1 November 2023) doubled this maximum to 10 years imprisonment. Any source material showing 5 years is now out of date. This doubling reflects the Oireachtas's recognition that assault causing harm (particularly in domestic violence contexts and serious street violence) required more severe sentencing powers. On summary conviction in the District Court, the penalty is a Class A fine and/or 12 months imprisonment. The offence is a hybrid offence, triable either way. Option A incorrectly states the pre-2023 maximum. Option B invents an intermediate amendment. Option D applies the s.4 life maximum to s.3.

questions.question: Karl punches Maria during a street altercation. Maria does not lose consciousness but suffers a broken jaw, causing her pain and requiring surgical intervention. Karl argues that Maria started the fight. Has the actus reus of s.3 assault causing harm been established?

options.A: No — Maria started the fight, so Karl was acting in self-defence and no assault is made out
options.B: Yes — Karl committed an assault (s.2 physical application of force) and that assault caused harm (broken jaw, pain, medical treatment required); the actus reus of s.3 is established; whether self-defence succeeds is a separate question
options.C: No — the harm must be permanent; a broken jaw can heal and therefore is not 'harm' under s.1
options.D: Yes — but only because medical treatment was required; without medical intervention, a broken jaw would not meet the s.1 definition of harm

questions.explanation: The actus reus of s.3 is clearly established on these facts: (1) Assault — Karl applied force to Maria's body by punching her (s.2 physical assault); (2) Harm — a broken jaw causing pain and requiring surgical intervention plainly falls within the s.1 definition of harm (harm to the body, including pain). The s.1 definition does not require permanent harm — temporary significant injury suffices. Whether Karl has a self-defence argument under s.18 NFOATP 1997 (as the sole governing provision following DPP v Crawford [2024] IESC 44) is a separate defence question that goes to liability, not to whether the elements of the offence are prima facie established. Option A conflates the elements of the offence with the defence. Option C incorrectly requires permanence. Option D adds a medical treatment requirement that has no statutory basis.

questions.question: In Minister for Justice v Donnelly [2012] IEHC, the High Court made an important clarification about the structure of s.3 NFOATP 1997. What did this decision establish?

options.A: The court held that s.3 creates a single offence that can be proved by either direct force or threatened force combined with harm
options.B: The court held that since s.3 incorporates the assault definition from s.2, and s.2 contains two distinct offences (physical assault and threatened assault), s.3 also contains two distinct offences which must be pleaded distinctly — the prosecution must specify which form of assault is alleged
options.C: The court held that s.3 is a strict liability offence — once harm is proved, mens rea is unnecessary
options.D: The court held that s.3 and s.4 are charged together as a composite offence in all cases

questions.explanation: Minister for Justice v Donnelly [2012] IEHC is significant procedurally: since s.3 NFOATP 1997 incorporates the assault definition from s.2, and since s.2 itself contains two distinct offences — (a) physical application of force, and (b) causing apprehension of immediate force — the court held that s.3 consequently also contains two distinct offences. The prosecution must plead specifically which form of assault is alleged (physical or threatened) combined with which form of harm resulted. This matters for the clarity of charges and the accused's ability to answer the case. Failure to plead distinctly may result in an ambiguous charge. Option A incorrectly allows a single composite pleading. Option C is wrong — s.3 expressly requires mens rea (the assault must be intentional or reckless). Option D has no basis in law.

questions.question: Emma and Simon engage in consensual sadomasochistic activity. Simon causes significant bodily harm to Emma, who has expressly consented to this treatment in advance. Simon is charged under s.3 NFOATP 1997. What is the Irish legal position on consent as a defence?

options.A: Consent is an absolute defence to s.3 — Emma's express prior consent is a complete answer to the charge
options.B: Following DPP v Brown [2023] IESC (Dunne J majority), consent is not a valid defence under s.3 where the harm is inflicted for an unlawful purpose; sadomasochistic harm for sexual gratification does not have a recognised lawful purpose; therefore Simon's conviction may stand despite Emma's consent
options.C: Consent is always irrelevant to s.3 — the physical harm automatically negates consent as a matter of law
options.D: Consent is available as a defence to s.3 only if both parties are married

questions.explanation: DPP v Brown [2023] IESC is the leading Irish authority on consent and harm. In Brown, the Supreme Court (per Dunne J, majority) held that consent is not a valid defence to s.3 assault causing harm where the harm is inflicted for an unlawful purpose. Sadomasochistic sexual activity causing significant bodily harm does not have a recognised lawful purpose in Irish law (such as legitimate surgery, sporting competition, or tattooing/piercing), and therefore consent does not negate criminal liability. This broadly aligns with the UK decision in R v Brown [1994] HOL, though the Irish Supreme Court approached the issue on public interest grounds. Minority views (Finlay Geoghegan J and McKechnie J) favoured greater weight to personal autonomy. Option A incorrectly treats consent as an absolute defence to s.3. Option C incorrectly says consent is always legally irrelevant. Option D invents a marital exception that has no statutory basis.

questions.question: How does s.3 assault causing harm differ from s.4 causing serious harm under the NFOATP 1997?

options.A: There is no substantive difference — s.3 and s.4 are interchangeable and the prosecution may charge either without distinction
options.B: The critical distinction is in the level of injury: s.3 requires 'harm' (defined broadly as pain, unconsciousness, or substantial impairment of physical or mental condition); s.4 requires 'serious harm' (a substantially higher threshold — injury creating substantial risk of death, serious disfigurement, or substantial loss of mobility or bodily function); s.4 carries life imprisonment; s.3 carries 10 years maximum
options.C: S.3 applies only to intentional assaults; s.4 applies to both intentional and reckless conduct
options.D: S.4 requires prior planning; s.3 applies to spontaneous violence

questions.explanation: The distinction between s.3 and s.4 turns on the degree of injury caused: (1) S.3 — 'harm' (s.1 definition): broad — pain, unconsciousness, substantial impairment of physical or mental condition; no permanence or severity required beyond what meets this definition; maximum now 10 years on indictment; (2) S.4 — 'serious harm' (s.1 definition): substantially higher threshold — injury that creates a substantial risk of death, causes serious disfigurement, or results in substantial loss or impairment of the mobility of the body or of the function of any bodily member or organ; maximum life imprisonment. Both s.3 and s.4 require the same mens rea (intentionally or recklessly), so Option C is incorrect. Option D invents a planning distinction that has no statutory basis. Option A is wrong — the two offences are distinct and carry very different penalties.

questions.question: S.3 assault causing harm is classified as a hybrid offence. What does this mean in practice?

options.A: It means the offence can only be charged in combination with another offence
options.B: It means the offence is triable either summarily (in the District Court, with consent of both the DPP and the satisfaction of the District Court that it is appropriate) or on indictment (in the Circuit Court); this gives flexibility in minor s.3 cases to be dealt with at summary level
options.C: It means the prosecution can choose the sentence it seeks without limitation
options.D: It means the offence always requires a jury trial, regardless of the level of harm

questions.explanation: A hybrid offence (also called a 'triable either way' offence) is one that can be tried either summarily (in the District Court without a jury) or on indictment (in the Circuit Court with a jury), depending on the circumstances. For s.3 to be tried summarily, two conditions must be met: (1) the DPP must consent to summary disposal; and (2) the District Court must be satisfied that the case is appropriate for summary disposal. On summary conviction, the maximum is a Class A fine and/or 12 months. On conviction on indictment, the maximum is 10 years (post-CJ(MP)A 2023). This structure gives the prosecution flexibility: less serious s.3 assaults can be resolved in the District Court, while more serious ones proceed to the Circuit Court. Options A, C, and D each misstate the meaning of a hybrid offence.

questions.question: An accused repeatedly slaps a flatmate over several weeks, causing bruising and significant psychological distress — the flatmate reports anxiety, disrupted sleep, and fear at home. The bruising heals within days. Can both physical harm and psychological harm separately constitute 'harm' under s.1 NFOATP 1997 for a s.3 charge?

options.A: No — psychological harm cannot constitute 'harm' under the NFOATP 1997; only physical injuries qualify
options.B: Yes — the s.1 definition of 'harm' expressly encompasses harm to the body or mind; psychological harm (anxiety, disrupted sleep, and substantial impairment of mental condition from repeated assault) constitutes harm under s.1; the physical bruising also constitutes harm even though temporary; either or both can ground a s.3 charge
options.C: No — the bruising healed within days; harm must be lasting for at least a month to constitute 'harm' under s.1
options.D: Yes — but only if the psychological harm was diagnosed by a consultant psychiatrist and certified in a medical report

questions.explanation: Section 1 NFOATP 1997 defines harm as 'harm to the body or mind' — explicitly including both physical and psychological injury. Anxiety, disrupted sleep, and persistent fear resulting from a pattern of assault can constitute substantial impairment of mental condition and therefore qualify as 'harm' for s.3 purposes. Similarly, temporary physical bruising causing pain satisfies the 'pain' or 'harm to the body' limb of s.1 — permanence is not required. On the facts, the prosecution has two independent harm bases: physical (bruising, pain) and psychological (anxiety, impaired sleep, fear). Multiple concurrent s.3 charges can be laid reflecting each incident. Option A incorrectly excludes psychological harm. Option C invents a minimum duration requirement. Option D adds a medical certification requirement that is not in the statute, though in practice medical evidence would assist in proving psychological harm to the jury.
